title: Figure skater

infobox.value: [Glossary of figure skating terms](/wiki/Glossary_of_figure_skating_terms "Glossary of figure skating terms")

Part of the Summer Olympics in [1908](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1908 Summer Olympics") and [1920](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1920 Summer Olympics"); Part of [the first Winter Olympics in 1924](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1924 Winter Olympics") to today

is a sport in which individuals, pairs, or groups perform on [figure skates](/wiki/Figure_skate "Figure skate") on ice. It was the first winter sport to be included in the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), with its introduction occurring at the [1908 Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1908 Summer Olympics") in London.[[1]](#cite_note-AFS2014-1) The Olympic disciplines are [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"),[[note 1]](#cite_note-3) [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"); the four individual disciplines are also combined into a team event, which was first included in the Winter Olympics in [2014](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics"). The non-Olympic disciplines include [synchronized skating](/wiki/Synchronized_skating "Synchronized skating"), [Theater on Ice](/wiki/Ice_theatre "Ice theatre"), and [four skating](/wiki/Four_skating "Four skating"). From intermediate through senior-level competition, skaters generally perform two programs (the [short program](/wiki/Short_program_(figure_skating) "Short program (figure skating)") and the [free skate](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating")), which, depending on the discipline, may include [spins](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins "Figure skating spins"), [jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps"), [moves in the field](/wiki/Moves_in_the_field "Moves in the field"), [lifts](/wiki/Figure_skating_lifts "Figure skating lifts"), [throw jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps#Throw_jumps "Figure skating jumps"), [death spirals](/wiki/Death_spiral_(figure_skating) "Death spiral (figure skating)"), and other elements or moves.

of the blade is on the side closest to the skater; the **outside edge** of the blade is on the side farthest from the skater. In figure skating, it is always desirable to skate on only one edge of the blade. Skating on both at the same time (which is referred to as a *flat*) may result in lower skating skills scores. The apparently effortless power and glide across the ice exhibited by elite figure skaters fundamentally derives from [efficient use of the edges to generate speed](/wiki/Conservation_of_angular_momentum#Conservation_of_angular_momentum "Conservation of angular momentum").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

on the front of each blade. The toepicks are mainly used to help launch the skater into the air for the take-off when performing jumps. Ice dance blades have smaller toepicks than blades used for the other disciplines.

of the blade, which is one of two rockers to be found on a blade and is the roundest portion of the blade. The sweet spot is located just behind the toe pick and near the middle of the blade. The other rocker is the more general curvature of the blade when stroking or gliding.

Ice sport performed on figure skates

**Figure skating** is a sport in which individuals, pairs, or groups perform on [figure skates](/wiki/Figure_skate "Figure skate") on ice. It was the first winter sport to be included in the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), with its introduction occurring at the [1908 Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1908 Summer Olympics") in London.[[1]](#cite_note-AFS2014-1) The Olympic disciplines are [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"),[[note 1]](#cite_note-3) [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"); the four individual disciplines are also combined into a team event, which was first included in the Winter Olympics in [2014](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics"). The non-Olympic disciplines include [synchronized skating](/wiki/Synchronized_skating "Synchronized skating"), [Theater on Ice](/wiki/Ice_theatre "Ice theatre"), and [four skating](/wiki/Four_skating "Four skating"). From intermediate through senior-level competition, skaters generally perform two programs (the [short program](/wiki/Short_program_(figure_skating) "Short program (figure skating)") and the [free skate](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating")), which, depending on the discipline, may include [spins](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins "Figure skating spins"), [jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps"), [moves in the field](/wiki/Moves_in_the_field "Moves in the field"), [lifts](/wiki/Figure_skating_lifts "Figure skating lifts"), [throw jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps#Throw_jumps "Figure skating jumps"), [death spirals](/wiki/Death_spiral_(figure_skating) "Death spiral (figure skating)"), and other elements or moves.

Figure skaters compete at various levels from beginner up to the Olympic level (senior) at local, regional, sectional, national, and international competitions. The [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU) regulates international figure skating judging and competitions. These include the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_Olympic_Games "Figure skating at the Olympic Games"), the [World Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships"), the [World Junior Championships](/wiki/World_Junior_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Junior Figure Skating Championships"), the [European Championships](/wiki/European_Figure_Skating_Championships "European Figure Skating Championships"), the [Four Continents Championships](/wiki/Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "Four Continents Figure Skating Championships"), the Grand Prix series ([senior](/wiki/ISU_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating "ISU Grand Prix of Figure Skating") and [junior](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "ISU Junior Grand Prix")), and the [ISU Challenger Series](/wiki/ISU_Challenger_Series "ISU Challenger Series").

The sport is also associated with show business. Major competitions generally conclude with exhibition galas, in which the top skaters from each discipline perform non-competitive programs. Many skaters, both during and after their competitive careers, also skate in ice shows, which run during the competitive season and the off-season.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/NSAPINY9_EXTR.jpg/250px-NSAPINY9_EXTR.jpg)](/wiki/File:NSAPINY9_EXTR.jpg)

"Central Park, Winter: the Skating Pond", 1862 lithograph

The term "professional" in skating refers not to skill level but competitive status. Figure skaters competing at the highest levels of international competition are not "professional" skaters. They are sometimes referred to as amateurs,[[3]](#cite_note-USAT110315-4) even though some earn money. Professional skaters include those who have lost their ISU eligibility and those who perform only in shows. They may also include former Olympic and World champions who have ended their competitive career, as well as skaters with little or no international competitive experience. In addition to performing in ice shows, professional skaters often compete in professional competitions, which are held throughout the world, each with its own format and rules.[[4]](#cite_note-MS220721-5)

The term "figure skating" is an English-language term; the sport is called *Eiskunstlauf* (lit. "Ice Art Run") in German and *patinage artistique* (lit. "artistic skating") in French.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xxx-6)

## Figure skates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Figure skates")]

Main article: [Figure skate](/wiki/Figure_skate "Figure skate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Figure-skates-2.jpg/250px-Figure-skates-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Figure-skates-2.jpg)

Close-up of a figure skating [blade](/wiki/Figure_skate#Blades "Figure skate"), showing the toe picks, the hollow (groove) on the bottom of the blade, and screw attachment

The most visible difference from [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") skates is that figure skates have a set of large, jagged teeth called *toe picks* on the front part of the [blade](/wiki/Figure_skate#Blades "Figure skate"). These are used primarily in jumping and should not be used for stroking or spins. If used during a spin, the toe pick will cause the skater to lose momentum, or move away from the center of the spin. Blades are mounted to the sole and heel of the boot with screws. Typically, high-level figure skaters are professionally fitted for their boots and blades at a reputable skate shop. Professionals are also employed to sharpen blades to individual requirements.[[6]](#cite_note-NYT090622-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-79630-0003%2C_Bodo_Bockenauer.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-79630-0003%2C_Bodo_Bockenauer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-79630-0003,_Bodo_Bockenauer.jpg)

Blade sharpening

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Figure_skating_edges.svg/250px-Figure_skating_edges.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Figure_skating_edges.svg)

Figure skates and [edges](/wiki/Glossary_of_figure_skating_terms#E "Glossary of figure skating terms")

Blades are about 4.7 millimetres (3⁄16 inch) thick. When viewed from the side, the blade of a figure skate is not flat, but curved slightly, forming an arc of a circle with a [radius](/wiki/Radius "Radius") of 180–220 centimetres (71–87 inches). This curvature is referred to as the *rocker* of the blade. The "sweet spot" is the part of the blade on which all spins are rotated; this is usually located near the [stanchion](/wiki/Stanchion "Stanchion") of the blade, below the ball of the foot.[[7]](#cite_note-fsequip-8) The blade is also "hollow ground"; a groove on the bottom of the blade creates two distinct [edges](/wiki/Glossary_of_figure_skating_terms#E "Glossary of figure skating terms"), inside and outside. The **inside edge** of the blade is on the side closest to the skater; the **outside edge** of the blade is on the side farthest from the skater. In figure skating, it is always desirable to skate on only one edge of the blade. Skating on both at the same time (which is referred to as a *flat*) may result in lower skating skills scores. The apparently effortless power and glide across the ice exhibited by elite figure skaters fundamentally derives from [efficient use of the edges to generate speed](/wiki/Conservation_of_angular_momentum#Conservation_of_angular_momentum "Conservation of angular momentum").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Skates used in singles and pair skating have a set of large, jagged teeth called a **toepick** on the front of each blade. The toepicks are mainly used to help launch the skater into the air for the take-off when performing jumps. Ice dance blades have smaller toepicks than blades used for the other disciplines.

During a spin, skaters use the **sweet spot** of the blade, which is one of two rockers to be found on a blade and is the roundest portion of the blade. The sweet spot is located just behind the toe pick and near the middle of the blade. The other rocker is the more general curvature of the blade when stroking or gliding.

Ice dancers' blades are about an inch shorter in the rear than those used by skaters in other disciplines, to accommodate the intricate footwork and close partnering in dance. Dancers' blades also have a smaller toepick as they do not require the large toepick used for jumping in the other disciplines. Hard plastic *skate guards* are used when the skater must walk in his or her skates when not on the ice, to protect the blade from dirt or material on the ground that may dull the blade. Soft blade covers called *soakers* are used to absorb condensation and protect the blades from rust when the skates are not being worn. In competition, skaters are allowed three minutes to make repairs to their skates.

There are many different types of boots and blades to suit different disciplines and abilities. For example, athletes who are performing advanced multi-rotational jumps often need a stiffer boot that is higher and gives more support. Athletes working on single or double jumps require less support and may use a less stiff boot. Ice dancers may prefer a lower cut boot that is designed to enable more knee bend.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Likewise, blades designed for free and pairs skating have a longer tail to assist landing. The blade profile and picks are designed to assist with spinning, jump entry, take-off, landing, and exit. Modern blade technology increasingly uses carbon fibre and materials other than steel to make blades lighter. These materials may also be more flexible and help cushion jump landings and be protective of young athletes' joints.[[8]](#cite_note-9) Ice dance blades have short tails to enable close foot work and reduce the risk of blade clash in close complex moves. They may also be thinner to assist with glide and fast changes of edge.

Off-ice training is the term for physical conditioning that takes place off the ice.[[9]](#cite_note-10) Besides regular physical exercise, skaters do walk-throughs of jumps off the ice to practice sufficient rotation and height of their jumps, and to practice consistency in landing on one foot. In 2020/2021 many athletes relied on a variety of off-ice training and conditioning methods due to rinks being closed due to COVID-19.

## Ice rinks and rink equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ice rinks and rink equipment")]

Main article: [Figure skating rink](/wiki/Figure_skating_rink "Figure skating rink")

Since 1980, all figure skating competitions must be held in completely covered and enclosed rinks. The rule was expanded to include practice rinks in 1984.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xxvii-11) According to figure skating historian James R. Hines, the development of indoor ice rinks, other than the development of the bladed skate during the 14th century and the practice of fastening boots permanently to skates in the 19th century, has had the greatest effect on figure skating. It allowed for skating year-round, as well as anywhere in the world, and prevented the cancellation of competitive events due to the lack of ice in outdoor rinks. The first attempts to make artificial ice occurred during the 1870s in England and the U.S. The first notable indoor ice rink was made in 1876, by [John Gamgee](/wiki/John_Gamgee "John Gamgee"), in [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea,_London "Chelsea, London") along the north bank of the [Thames River](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames"); it measured 24 by 40 feet. By the end of the 19th century, many major cities in Europe and North America had indoor rinks.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines201123–24-12)

There is significant variation in the dimensions of ice rinks. Olympic-sized rinks have dimensions of 30 m × 60 m (98.4 ft × 197 ft), [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")-sized rinks are 26 m × 61 m (85 ft × 200 ft), while European rinks are sometimes 30 m × 64 m (98 ft × 210 ft).[[12]](#cite_note-aboutrinks-13) The [ISU](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") prefers Olympic-sized rinks for figure skating competitions, particularly for major events. According to ISU rule 342, a figure skating rink for an ISU event "if possible, shall measure sixty (60) meters in one direction and thirty (30) meters in the other, but not larger, and not less than fifty-six (56) meters in one direction and twenty-six (26) meters in the other."[[13]](#cite_note-isuSFTR-14) The scoring system rewards skaters who have good ice coverage, i.e. those who efficiently cover the entire ice surface during their programs. Olympic-sized rinks make the differences in skill between skaters more apparent but they are not available for all events. If a rink has different dimensions, a skater's jump setup and speed may be hindered as he or she adjusts.[[14]](#cite_note-AP111022-15)[[15]](#cite_note-IN131007-16)

Ice quality is judged by smoothness, friction, hardness, and brittleness.[[16]](#cite_note-WMQI1105-17) Factors affecting ice quality include temperature, water quality, and usage, with toe picks causing more deterioration. For figure skating, the ice surface temperature is normally maintained between −5.5 and −3.5 °C (22.1 and 25.7 °F),[[16]](#cite_note-WMQI1105-17)[[17]](#cite_note-Olympic.org161031-18) with the Olympic disciplines requiring slightly softer ice (−3.5 °C) than synchronized skating (−5.5 °C).[[18]](#cite_note-19) Typically after every two warm-up groups, an [ice resurfacer](/wiki/Ice_resurfacer "Ice resurfacer") cleans and smooths the surface of the ice sheet. Inadequate ice quality may affect skaters' performances.[[19]](#cite_note-GS120328ps-20)

Some rinks have a harness system installed to help skaters learn new jumps in a controlled manner. A heavy-duty cable is securely attached to two of the walls around the ice, with a set of pulleys riding on the cable. The skater wears a vest or belt, with a cable or rope attached to it, and the cable/rope is threaded through the movable pulley on the cable above. The coach holds the other end of the cable and lifts the skater by pulling the cable/rope. The skater can then practice the jump with the coach assisting the completion. This is used when a skater needs more help on a jump. However, if the coaches see fit, they could use another harness usually called "the fishing pole harness." It is named that because it looks similar to a fishing pole. The skater will put on the harness and the coach will adjust it so it fits the skater. The skater will go and do the jump with very little help from their coach. They can also do the jump on any pattern they choose, whereas, the other harness, they must do in a straight line.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

## Disciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Disciplines")]

Figure skating consists of the following disciplines:

* In **[single skating](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating")**, male and female skaters compete individually. Figure skating is the oldest winter sport contested at the Olympics, with men's and women's single skating appearing as two of the four figure skating events at the [London Games in 1908](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics").[[21]](#cite_note-22) Single skating has required elements that skaters must perform during a competition and that make up a well-balanced skating program. They include [jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps") (and jump combinations), [spins](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins "Figure skating spins"), [step sequences](/wiki/Step_sequences "Step sequences"), and [choreographic sequences](/wiki/Choreographic_sequence "Choreographic sequence").[[22]](#cite_note-S&P/ID2022-112-23)
* **[Pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating")** is defined as "the skating of two persons in unison who perform their movements in such harmony with each other as to give the impression of genuine Pair Skating as compared with independent Single Skating".[[23]](#cite_note-ISU2022-109-24) The ISU also states that a pairs team consists of "one Woman and one Man".[[24]](#cite_note-ISU2021-9-25) Pair skating, along with men's and women's single skating, has been an Olympic discipline since figure skating, the oldest Winter Olympic sport, was introduced at the [1908 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics") in London.[[25]](#cite_note-26) The [ISU World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/1908_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1908 World Figure Skating Championships") introduced pair skating in 1908.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Pair skating required elements include [lifts](/wiki/Figure_skating_lifts#Pair_lifts "Figure skating lifts"), twist lifts, [jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps#Jumps_in_pair_skating "Figure skating jumps") and partner assisted jumps, [pair spins](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins#Pair_skating "Figure skating spins"), [death spirals](/wiki/Death_spiral_(figure_skating) "Death spiral (figure skating)"), [step sequences](/wiki/Step_sequence "Step sequence"), and [choreographic sequences](/wiki/Choreographic_sequence "Choreographic sequence").[[27]](#cite_note-28) The elements performed by pairs teams must be "linked together by connecting steps of a different nature"[[28]](#cite_note-ISU2022-1093-29) and by other comparable movements and with a variety of holds and positions.
* **[Ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance")** historically draws from [ballroom dancing](/wiki/Ballroom_dancing "Ballroom dancing"). It joined the [World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") in 1952, and became a [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") medal sport in 1976.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKestnbaum2003223-30) According to the ISU, an ice dance team consists of one woman and one man.[[24]](#cite_note-ISU2021-9-25) Ice dance has its roots in the "combined skating" developed in the 19th century by skating clubs and organizations and in recreational social skating.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKestnbaum2003xiv,_102-31) The first national competitions occurred in England, Canada, the U.S., and Austria during the 1930s.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011102-32) The first international ice dance competition took place as a special event at the [World Championships in 1950](/wiki/1950_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1950 World Figure Skating Championships") in London.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011173–174-33) The elements ice dance teams must perform are the dance [lift](/wiki/Figure_skating_lifts "Figure skating lifts"), the dance [spin](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins "Figure skating spins"), the [step sequence](/wiki/Step_sequence "Step sequence"), [twizzles](/wiki/Twizzle "Twizzle"), and choreographic elements.[[33]](#cite_note-34)
* **[Synchronized skating](/wiki/Synchronized_skating "Synchronized skating")** (formerly known as "precision skating") is for mixed-gender groups of between twelve and twenty figure skaters. This discipline resembles a group form of ice dance, with additional emphasis on precise formations of the group as a whole and complex transitions between formations. The basic formations include wheels, blocks, lines, circles, and intersections. The close formations, and the need for the team to stay in unison, add to the difficulty of the footwork performed by the skaters in these elements. Formal proposals were put forward by the [ISU](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") to include synchronized skating in the 2022 Winter Olympics, but those efforts have been unsuccessful.[[34]](#cite_note-CT140929-35)[[35]](#cite_note-NBC180224-36)
* **[Compulsory figures](/wiki/Compulsory_figures "Compulsory figures")** or **school figures** were formerly a discipline of figure skating, and gave the sport its name. They are the "circular patterns which skaters trace on the ice to demonstrate skill in placing clean turns evenly on round circles".[[36]](#cite_note-37) For approximately the first 50 years of figure skating as a sport, until 1947, compulsory figures made up 60 percent of the total score at most competitions around the world. These figures continued to dominate the sport, although they steadily declined in importance, until the ISU voted to discontinue them as a part of competitions in 1990.[[37]](#cite_note-38) Since 2015 with the founding of the World Figure Sport Society and the World Figure & Fancy Skating Championships & Festival on black ice, more skaters are training and competing in figures.[[38]](#cite_note-39) More coaches are learning the new methods developed by World Figure Sport to teach them to skaters, as some skaters and coaches believe that figures give skaters an advantage in developing alignment, core strength, body control, and discipline.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

## Elements and moves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Elements and moves")]

Main article: [Glossary of figure skating terms](/wiki/Glossary_of_figure_skating_terms "Glossary of figure skating terms")

Each element receives a score according to its base value and grade of execution (GOE), resulting in a combined technical elements score (TES). At competitions, a technical specialist identifies the elements and assigns each one a level of difficulty, ranging from B (Basic) to Level 4 (most difficult).[[40]](#cite_note-isu2168-41) For each element, a panel of judges determines the GOE, ranging between −5 and +5, according to how well the skater executes the element. The GOE is weighted according to the base value of the element.[[40]](#cite_note-isu2168-41) Through the ISU guidelines skaters must perform a minimum of seven elements in their short program and twelve elements in their long program.[[13]](#cite_note-isuSFTR-14)

The ISU defines a fall as a loss of control with the result that the majority of the skater's body weight is not on the blade but supported by hands, knees, or buttocks.[[41]](#cite_note-deduc-42)

### Jumps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Jumps")]

Main article: [Figure skating jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps")

| ISU abbreviations: [Jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps") | |
| --- | --- |
| T | [Toe loop](/wiki/Toe_loop_jump "Toe loop jump") |
| S | [Salchow](/wiki/Salchow_jump "Salchow jump") |
| Lo | [Loop](/wiki/Loop_jump "Loop jump") |
| F | [Flip](/wiki/Flip_jump "Flip jump") |
| Lz | [Lutz](/wiki/Lutz_jump "Lutz jump") |
| A | [Axel](/wiki/Axel_jump "Axel jump") |

Jumps involve the skater leaping into the air and rotating rapidly to land after completing one or more rotations. There are many types of jumps, identified by the way the skater takes off and lands, as well as by the number of rotations that are completed.

Each jump receives a score according to its base value and grade of execution (GOE).[[40]](#cite_note-isu2168-41) Quality of execution, technique, height, speed, flow and ice coverage are considered by the judges. An *under-rotated* jump (indicated by < ) is "missing rotation of more than 1⁄4, but less than 1⁄2 revolution" and receives 70% of the base value. A *downgraded* jump (indicated by << ) is "missing rotation of 1⁄2 revolution or more". A downgraded triple is treated as a double jump, while a downgraded double is treated as a single jump.

An edge violation occurs when a skater executes a jump on the incorrect edge. The hollow is a groove on the bottom of the [blade](/wiki/Figure_skate#Blades "Figure skate") which creates two distinct edges, inside and outside. The inside edge of the blade is on the side closest to the skater, the outside edge is on the side farthest from the skater, and a *flat* refers to skating on both edges at the same time, which is discouraged. An unclear edge or edge violation is indicated with an 'e' and reflected in the GOE according to the severity of the problem. *Flutz* and *lip* are the colloquial terms for a Lutz and flip jump with an edge violation.

In 1982, the [ISU](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") enacted a rule stating that a skater may perform each type of triple only once in a program, or twice if one of them is incorporated into a combination or sequence. For a set of jumps to be considered a *combination*, each jump must take off from the landing edge of the previous jump, with no steps, turns, or change of edge between jumps. Toe loops and loops are commonly performed as the second or third jump in a combination because they take off from the back outside edge of the landing foot, or skating leg. To perform a salchow or flip on the back end of a combination, a half loop (which is actually a full rotation, but lands on a back inside edge of the landing leg) may be used as a connecting jump. In contrast, jump *sequences* are sets of jumps that may be linked by non-listed jumps or hops.[[42]](#cite_note-tphss-43) Sequences are worth 80% of the combined value of the same jumps executed in combination.

Video demonstrating basic figure skating jumps

A figure skater only needs to be able to jump in one direction, either clockwise or counter-clockwise. The vast majority of figure skaters prefer to rotate in a counter-clockwise direction when jumping. Thus, for clarity, all jumps will be described for a skater jumping counter-clockwise.

There are six jumps in figure skating that count as jump elements. All six are landed on one foot on the back outside edge (with counter-clockwise rotation, for single and multi-revolution jumps), but have different takeoffs, by which they may be distinguished. Jumps are divided into two different categories: [*toe jumps*](#Toe_jumps) and [*edge jumps*](#Edge_jumps).

The number of rotations performed in the air determines whether the jump is a single, double, triple, or quadruple (commonly known as a "[quad](/wiki/Quad_(figure_skating) "Quad (figure skating)")"). The simplest jump is a waltz jump, which can only be done in a half-leap and is not classified as a single, double, or triple jump. Triple jumps, other than the triple Axel, are commonly performed by female single skaters. It is rare for a female skater to land a quadruple jump, and very few female single skaters have been credited with quads in competition. Senior-level male single skaters perform mostly triple and quadruple jumps in competition. The final of the six jumps to be landed cleanly as a quad in international competition is the elusive [quadruple Axel](/wiki/Axel_jump "Axel jump"). A few male skaters made valiant efforts to land the immensely difficult four-and-a-half revolution jump (most notably two-time Olympic Champion from Japan, [Yuzuru Hanyu](/wiki/Yuzuru_Hanyu "Yuzuru Hanyu")), but failed to land one cleanly and fully-rotated. The first clean and fully-rotated quad Axel was successfully landed by American men's skater [Ilia Malinin](/wiki/Ilia_Malinin "Ilia Malinin") at the [2022 CS U.S. Classic](/wiki/2022_CS_U.S._International_Figure_Skating_Classic "2022 CS U.S. International Figure Skating Classic"),[[43]](#cite_note-44) 34 years after the first-ever quadruple jump (a quad toe loop) was landed by Canada's [Kurt Browning](/wiki/Kurt_Browning "Kurt Browning") at the World Figure Skating Championships in 1988.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

The takeoff speed of a jump can reach up to 25 kilometers per hour.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Prior to most jumps, a figure skater needs to skate backward to build power and speed.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

#### Toe jumps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Toe jumps")]

Toe jumps are launched by digging the toe pick of one skate into the ice, using it to vault into the air with the opposite leg. The main toe jumps are (in order of score value):[[47]](#cite_note-48)

1. [Toe loop](/wiki/Toe_loop_jump "Toe loop jump") – the skater takes off backwards from the outside edge of the right (or left) foot, launching the jump using the opposite toe pick.
2. [Flip](/wiki/Flip_jump "Flip jump") (sometimes known as a toe salchow) – the skater takes off backwards from the inside edge of the left (or right) foot and assists the take-off using the opposite toe pick.
3. [Lutz](/wiki/Lutz_jump "Lutz jump") – similar to the flip, but the skater takes off from the backward *outside* edge of the left (or right) foot, launching the jump using the opposite toe pick.

All of the above descriptions assume a counter-clockwise direction of rotation, landing backwards on the outside edge of the right foot. (For clockwise rotation, the skater takes off using the alternative foot and lands backwards on the outside edge of the left foot.)

#### Edge jumps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Edge jumps")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Axel_Paulsen_jump_without_text.svg/330px-Axel_Paulsen_jump_without_text.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Axel_Paulsen_jump_without_text.svg)

An Axel jump

Edge jumps use no toe assist, and include (in order of score value):

1. [Salchow](/wiki/Salchow_jump "Salchow jump") – the skater takes off backwards from the inside edge of the left (or right) foot, allowing the edge to come round, the opposite leg helps to launch the jump into the air.
2. [Loop](/wiki/Loop_jump "Loop jump") (also known as a Rittberger jump) – the skater takes off backwards from the outside edge of the right (or left) foot.
3. [Axel](/wiki/Axel_jump "Axel jump") – the skater takes off forwards from the outside edge of the left (or right) foot. As this is the only rotating jump to take off from a forward edge, it includes an extra half rotation.

Again, these descriptions assume a counter-clockwise direction of rotation, landing backwards on the outside edge of the right foot. (For clockwise rotation, the skater takes off using the alternative foot and always lands backwards on the outside edge of the left foot.)

#### Other jumps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other jumps")]

Several other jumps are usually performed only as single jumps and in elite skating are used as transitional movements or highlights in step sequences. These include the half toe loop (ballet jump), half loop, half flip, [walley jump](/wiki/Walley_jump "Walley jump"), [waltz jump](/wiki/Waltz_jump "Waltz jump"), inside Axel, one-foot Axel, stag jump, and [split jump](/wiki/Split_jump "Split jump"). There are two kinds of split jump:

* Russian split, performed in a position that is similar to that of a straddle split
* front split, performed in the position of the more traditional split, facing the direction of the front leg

### Spins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Spins")]

Main article: [Figure skating spins](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins "Figure skating spins")

**Spins** are an element in which the skater rotates, centered on a single point on the ice, while holding one or more body positions.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetkevich1988127-49) They are performed by all disciplines of the sport.[[49]](#cite_note-50) As *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* says, "While jumps look like sport, spins look more like art. While jumps provide the suspense, spins provide the scenery, but there is so much more to the scenery than most viewers have time or means to grasp".[[50]](#cite_note-clarey-51) According to world champion and figure skating commentator [Scott Hamilton](/wiki/Scott_Hamilton_(figure_skater) "Scott Hamilton (figure skater)"), spins are often used "as breathing points or transitions to bigger things"[[50]](#cite_note-clarey-51)

Figure skating spins, along with jumps, spirals, and spread eagles were originally individual [compulsory figures](/wiki/Compulsory_figures "Compulsory figures"), sometimes special figures. Unlike jumps, spins were a "graceful and appreciated"[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011103-52) part of figure skating throughout the 19th century. They advanced between [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"); by the late 1930s, all three basic spin positions were used.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011103-52)

There are two types of spins, the **forward spin** and the **backward spin**.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetkevich1988129-53) There are three basic spin positions: the **[upright spin](/wiki/Upright_spin "Upright spin")**, the **[sit spin](/wiki/Sit_spin "Sit spin")**, and the **[camel spin](/wiki/Camel_spin "Camel spin")**. Skaters also perform **flying spins** and **combination spins**.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKestnbaum2003280-54)

### Lifts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Lifts")]

Main article: [Figure skating lifts](/wiki/Figure_skating_lifts "Figure skating lifts")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Maria_Mukhortova_and_Maxim_Trankov_at_2010_European_Championships_%283%29.jpg/250px-Maria_Mukhortova_and_Maxim_Trankov_at_2010_European_Championships_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maria_Mukhortova_and_Maxim_Trankov_at_2010_European_Championships_(3).jpg)

Pair skaters performing a one-arm overhead lift

**Figure skating lifts** are required elements in pair skating and ice dance.[[54]](#cite_note-55) There are five groups of **lifts in pair skating**, categorized in order of increasing level of difficulty.[[23]](#cite_note-ISU2022-109-24) Judges look for the following when evaluating pair lifts: speed of entry and exit; control of the woman's free leg when she is exiting out of the lift, with the goal of keeping the leg high and sweeping; the position of the woman in the air; the man's footwork; quick and easy changes of position; and the maintenance of flow throughout the lift.[[55]](#cite_note-skatecanada-56) **Twist lifts** are "the most thrilling and exciting component in pair skating".[[55]](#cite_note-skatecanada-56) They can also be the most difficult movement to perform correctly.[[56]](#cite_note-57) They require more strength and coordination than many other pair elements, and are usually the first or second element in a program. According to the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), "the Woman must be caught in the air at the waist by the Man prior to landing and be assisted to a smooth landing on the ice on a backward outside edge on one foot"[[57]](#cite_note-58) during a twist lift.

The ISU defines **dance lifts** as "a movement in which one of the partners is elevated with active and/or passive assistance of the other partner to any permitted height, sustained there and set down on the ice".[[58]](#cite_note-ISU2021-126-59) Dance lifts are delineated from pair lifts to ensure that ice dance and pair skating remain separate disciplines.[[59]](#cite_note-hostilerules2-60) After the judging system changed from the [6.0 system](/wiki/6.0_system "6.0 system") to the [ISU Judging System](/wiki/ISU_Judging_System "ISU Judging System") (IJS), dance lifts became more "athletic, dramatic and exciting".[[60]](#cite_note-dangerous-61) There are two types of dance lifts: **short lifts**, which should be done in under seven seconds; and **combination lifts**, which should be done in under 12 seconds.[[58]](#cite_note-ISU2021-126-59) A well-balanced [free dance program](/wiki/Free_dance_(ice_dance) "Free dance (ice dance)") in ice dance must include dance lifts.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

### Turns, steps, moves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Turns, steps, moves")]

Along with other forms of [skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating"), figure skating is one of the only [human powered](/wiki/Human-powered_transport "Human-powered transport") activities where travelling backwards is integral to the discipline. The ability to skate well backwards and forwards are considered to be equally important, as is the ability to transition well between the two.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

Step sequences are a required element in all four Olympic disciplines. The pattern can be straight line, circular, or serpentine. The step sequence consists of a combination of turns, steps, hops and edge changes. Additionally, steps and turns can be used as transitions between elements. The various turns, which skaters can incorporate into step sequences, include:

* [![Three-turn: the blade turns into the curve of the edge or lobe.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Threeturn.GIF/120px-Threeturn.GIF)](/wiki/File:Threeturn.GIF "Three-turn: the blade turns into the curve of the edge or lobe.")

  [Bracket turn](/wiki/Bracket_turn "Bracket turn"): the blade is turned counter to the curve of the lobe.
* [![Mohawk: the two-foot equivalent](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Mohawk-trace.png/120px-Mohawk-trace.png)](/wiki/File:Mohawk-trace.png "Mohawk: the two-foot equivalent")

  [Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_turn "Mohawk turn"): the two-foot equivalent

* [![Rocker: one-foot turn involving a change of lobe as well as direction](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Rocker_turn.gif/120px-Rocker_turn.gif)](/wiki/File:Rocker_turn.gif "Rocker: one-foot turn involving a change of lobe as well as direction")

  [Rocker](/wiki/Rocker_turn "Rocker turn"): one-foot turn involving a change of lobe as well as direction

  [Counter](/wiki/Counter_turn "Counter turn"): one-foot turn involving a change of lobe as well as direction
* [![Twizzles: traveling multi-rotation turns on one foot](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/2011_WFSC_2d_259_Siobhan_Heekin-Canedy_Alexander_Shakalov.JPG/120px-2011_WFSC_2d_259_Siobhan_Heekin-Canedy_Alexander_Shakalov.JPG)](/wiki/File:2011_WFSC_2d_259_Siobhan_Heekin-Canedy_Alexander_Shakalov.JPG "Twizzles: traveling multi-rotation turns on one foot")

  [Twizzles](/wiki/Twizzle "Twizzle"): traveling multi-rotation turns on one foot

[Choctaws](/wiki/Choctaw_turn "Choctaw turn") are the two-foot equivalents of rockers and counters. Other movements that may be incorporated into step sequences or used as connecting elements include lunges and [spread eagles](/wiki/Spread_eagle_(figure_skating) "Spread eagle (figure skating)"). An [Ina Bauer](/wiki/Ina_Bauer_(element) "Ina Bauer (element)") is similar to a spread eagle performed with one knee bent and typically an arched back. [Hydroblading](/wiki/Hydroblading "Hydroblading") refers to a deep edge performed with the body as low as possible to the ice in a near-horizontal position.

[Moves in the field](/wiki/Moves_in_the_field "Moves in the field") is a pre-determined required sequence that demonstrated basic skating skills and edge control. In the context of a competitive program, they include sequences that may include [spirals](/wiki/Spiral_(figure_skating) "Spiral (figure skating)"), [spread eagles](/wiki/Spread_eagle_(figure_skating) "Spread eagle (figure skating)"), [Ina Bauers](/wiki/Ina_Bauer_(element) "Ina Bauer (element)"), [hydroblading](/wiki/Hydroblading "Hydroblading"), and similar extended edge moves, along with loops, twizzles, and different kinds of turns.

A spiral is an element in which the skater moves across the ice on a specific edge with the free leg held at hip level or above. Spirals are distinguished by the edge of the blade used (inside or outside), the direction of motion (forward or backward), and the skater's position. A spiral sequence is one or more spiral positions and edges done in sequence. Judges look at the depth, stability, and control of the skating edge, speed and ice coverage, extension, and other factors. Some skaters can change edges during a spiral, i.e. from inside to outside edge. Spirals performed on a "flat" are generally not considered as true spirals. Spiral sequences were required in women's and pair skating prior to the 2012–13 season,[[64]](#cite_note-65) but from the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_figure_skating_season "2012–13 figure skating season") onward, they were replaced by the choreographic sequence. The choreographic sequence consists of moves in the field, unlisted jumps, spinning movements, etc. and is required for the men's, women's and pair free program.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)

A [death spiral](/wiki/Death_spiral_(figure_skating) "Death spiral (figure skating)") is a required element of pair skating. There are four varieties distinguished by the lady's edge and direction of motion. The man performs a [pivot](/wiki/Glossary_of_figure_skating_terms#P "Glossary of figure skating terms"), one toe anchored in the ice, while holding the hand of his partner, who circles him on a deep edge with her body almost parallel to the ice. As of 2011, the woman's head must at some time reach her skating knee. The man must also be in a full pivot position and the death spiral must be held for a minimum amount of rotation, depending on the level.

## Competition format and scoring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Competition format and scoring")]

Main articles: [Figure skating season](/wiki/Figure_skating_season "Figure skating season") and [Figure skating competition](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Riku_Miura_and_Ryuichi_Kihara_performing_a_cantilever_during_their_short_program_at_the_2024-25_Grand_Prix_Final.jpg/250px-Riku_Miura_and_Ryuichi_Kihara_performing_a_cantilever_during_their_short_program_at_the_2024-25_Grand_Prix_Final.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riku_Miura_and_Ryuichi_Kihara_performing_a_cantilever_during_their_short_program_at_the_2024-25_Grand_Prix_Final.jpg)

Pair Skaters [Riku Miura](/wiki/Riku_Miura "Riku Miura") and [Ryuichi Kihara](/wiki/Ryuichi_Kihara "Ryuichi Kihara") performing a cantilever during their short program at the [2024-25 Grand Prix Final](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "2024–25 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final")

The [ISU](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") is the governing body for international competitions in figure skating, including the World Championships and the figure skating events at the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games"). Medals are awarded for overall results; the standard medals are [gold](/wiki/Gold_medal "Gold medal") for first place, [silver](/wiki/Silver_medal "Silver medal") for second, and [bronze](/wiki/Bronze_medal "Bronze medal") for third place. [U.S. Figure Skating](/wiki/U.S._Figure_Skating "U.S. Figure Skating") also awards [pewter](/wiki/Pewter "Pewter") medals for fourth-place finishers in national events. Additionally, at the World, European, Four Continents, and World Junior Championships, the ISU awards *small medals* for segment results (short and free program) (Since 2009). A medal is generally attributed to only one country, even if a partnership is composed of skaters with different nationalities. A notable exception was the pair skating partnership between [Ludowika Eilers](/wiki/Ludowika_Eilers "Ludowika Eilers") and [Walter Jakobsson](/wiki/Walter_Jakobsson "Walter Jakobsson"); their 1910–11 medals were attributed to both Germany and Finland.[[68]](#cite_note-WCP-69) Beyond the early 20th century, no skaters have been allowed to represent two countries in the same competition.

In singles and pairs figure skating competition, competitors perform two programs: the [short program](/wiki/Short_program_(figure_skating) "Short program (figure skating)"), in which they complete a set of required elements consisting of jumps, spins and steps; and the [free skate](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating"), also known as the *long program*, in which they have a slightly wider choice of elements. Under both the 6.0 system and the ISU Judging System, the judges consider the "complete package" when evaluating performances, i.e. the best jumper is not always placed first if the judges consider the difference in jumping execution to be outweighed by another skater's speed, spins, presentation, etc.[[69]](#cite_note-nyt970216-70)[[70]](#cite_note-CBS981202-71)

Ice dance competitions formerly consisted of three phases: one or more [compulsory dances](/wiki/Compulsory_dance_(ice_dance) "Compulsory dance (ice dance)"); an [original dance](/wiki/Original_dance "Original dance") to a [ballroom](/wiki/Ballroom_dance "Ballroom dance") rhythm that was designated annually; and a [free dance](/wiki/Free_dance_(figure_skating) "Free dance (figure skating)") to music of the skaters' own choice. Beginning in the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_figure_skating_season "2010–2011 figure skating season"), the compulsory and original dances were merged into the [short dance](/wiki/Short_dance "Short dance"), which itself was renamed the *[rhythm dance](/wiki/Rhythm_dance "Rhythm dance")* in June 2018, before the [2018–19 season](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_figure_skating_season "2018–19 figure skating season").

### Medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Medals")]

Source:[[71]](#cite_note-72)

#### Overall Medals (Stage 1 + Stage 2)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Overall Medals (Stage 1 + Stage 2)")]

Medals awarded to the skaters who achieved the highest overall placements in each discipline.

#### Small Medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Small Medals")]

Small Medals awarded only at ISU Championships since probably 2009:[[72]](#cite_note-73)

**Stage 1 =** Small medals awarded to the skaters who achieved the highest [short program](/wiki/Short_Program_(figure_skating) "Short Program (figure skating)") or [rhythm dance](/wiki/Rhythm_dance "Rhythm dance") placements in each discipline.

**Stage 2 =** Small medals awarded to the skaters who achieved the highest [free skating](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating") or [free dance](/wiki/Free_dance_(figure_skating) "Free dance (figure skating)") placements in each discipline.

Small Medals awarded only at ISU Championships:

1. [World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships")
2. [World Junior Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Junior_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Junior Figure Skating Championships")
3. [European Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/European_Figure_Skating_Championships "European Figure Skating Championships")
4. [Four Continents Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "Four Continents Figure Skating Championships")

Small Medals not awarded in:
