2. [ISU Grand Prix of Figure Skating](/wiki/ISU_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating "ISU Grand Prix of Figure Skating")
3. or any other international competitions

### 6.0 System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 6.0 System")]

Main article: [6.0 system](/wiki/6.0_system "6.0 system")

Skating was formerly judged for "technical merit" (in the free skate), "required elements" (in the short program), and "presentation" (in both programs).[[70]](#cite_note-CBS981202-71) The marks for each program ran from 0.0 to 6.0, the latter being the highest. These marks were used to determine a preference ranking (or "ordinal") separately for each judge; the judges' preferences were then combined to determine placements for each skater in each program. The placements for the two programs were then combined, with the free skate placement weighted more heavily than the short program. The highest placing individual (based on the sum of the weighted placements) was declared the winner.[[73]](#cite_note-6.0-74)

### ISU Judging System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: ISU Judging System")]

Main article: [ISU Judging System](/wiki/ISU_Judging_System "ISU Judging System")

In 2004, in response to the [judging controversy](/wiki/2002_Olympic_Winter_Games_figure_skating_scandal "2002 Olympic Winter Games figure skating scandal") during the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics"), the ISU adopted the International Judging System (IJS), which became mandatory at all international competitions in 2006, including the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"). The new system is sometimes informally referred to as the *Code of Points*, however, the ISU has never used the term to describe their system in any of their official communications.

Under the IJS, points are awarded individually for each skating element, and the sum of these points is the *total element score* (TES). Competitive programs are constrained to include a set number of elements. Each element is judged first by a technical specialist who identifies the specific element and determines its *base value*. This is done using instant replay video to verify features that distinguish different elements; e.g. the exact foot position at take-off and landing of a jump. A panel of nine judges then each award a mark for the quality and execution of the element. This mark, called the *grade of execution* (GOE), is an integer with a minimum value of −5 and a maximum value of +5.[[40]](#cite_note-isu2168-41) The GOE mark is then translated into another value by using the table of values in ISU rule 322. The highest and lowest values are discarded, and the average is calculated. This average value is then added to (or subtracted from) the base value to determine the total value for the element. Previously, the GOE marks from the nine judges were processed with a computerized random selection and the average of the remaining seven was calculated.[[74]](#cite_note-judging-75)

Note: The IJS previously used a GOE scale of −3 to +3, but this was changed for the [2018–19 season](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_figure_skating_season "2018–19 figure skating season").[[75]](#cite_note-isu2089-76)

The *program components score* (PCS) awards points to holistic aspects of a program or other nuances that are not rewarded in the *total element score*. The components are:

:   * ***Composition*** : This evaluates how the program is designed in relation to the music; how are the different elements connected; how is the available space used; how does the choreography reflect musical phrase and form?

:   * ***Presentation***: This evaluates how the program is performed; what does the skater express and project; what energy is created; what is the musical sensitivity and timing; for Pair, Ice Dance and Synchronized skating is the skating appropriately synchronized and showing awareness of space?

:   * ***Skating skills***: This mark assesses the skater's command of the blade over the ice, including the ability to skate with power and ease. The judges look at variety and clarity of edges, balance, body control, turns, steps, flow, power and speed.[[76]](#cite_note-ISU_Media_Guide_23-24-77)[[77]](#cite_note-US_Rulebook_22-23-78)

A detailed description of each component is given in ISU rule 322.2. Judges award each component a raw mark from 0 to 10 in increments of 0.25, with a mark of 5 being defined as "average". For each separate component, the raw marks are then selected, trimmed, and averaged in a manner akin to determining a *grade of execution*. The trimmed mean scores are then translated into a factored mark by multiplying by a factor that depends on the discipline, competition segment, and level. Then the five (or four) factored marks are added to give the final PCS score.

The *total element score* and the *program components score* are added to give the total score for a competition segment (TSS). A skater's final placement is determined by the total of their scores in all segments of a competition. No ordinal rankings are used to determine the final results.

### Other judging and competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Other judging and competition")]

There are also skating competitions organized for professional skaters by independent promoters. These competitions use judging rules set by whoever organizes the competition. There is no "professional league". Well-known professional competitions in the past have included the [World Professional Championships](/wiki/World_Professional_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Professional Figure Skating Championships"), the Challenge Of Champions, and the Canadian Professional Championships.

The [Ice Skating Institute](/wiki/Ice_Skating_Institute "Ice Skating Institute") (ISI), an international ice rink trade organization, runs its own competitive and test program aimed at recreational skaters. Originally headquartered in Minnesota, the organization now operates out of Dallas, Texas. ISI competitions are open to any member that have registered their tests. There are very few "qualifying" competitions, although some districts hold Gold Competitions for that season's first-place winners. ISI competitions are especially popular in Asian countries that do not have established ISU member federations. The [Gay Games](/wiki/Gay_Games "Gay Games") have also included skating competitions for same-gender pairs and dance couples under ISI sponsorship. Other figure skating competitions for adults also attract participants from diverse cultures.

## World standings and season's bests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: World standings and season's bests")]

### World standings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: World standings")]

Main article: [ISU World Standings and Season's World Ranking](/wiki/ISU_World_Standings_and_Season%27s_World_Ranking "ISU World Standings and Season's World Ranking")

The **world standing (WS)** of a skater/couple is calculated based on the results over the current and preceding two seasons. Competitors receive points based on their final placement at an event and the event's weight. The following events receive points:[[78]](#cite_note-isuws-79)

* ISU Championships (World, European, Four Continents, and World Junior Championships) and Olympic Winter Games: The best result by points per season, the best two results by points over the three seasons.
* ISU Grand Prix of Figure Skating and Final (senior and junior): The two best results by points per season, the best four results by points over the three seasons.
* International senior calendar competitions: The two best results by points per season, the best four results by points over the three seasons.

Following the current season's World Championships, the results from the earliest season are deleted. A new partnership starts with zero points; there is no transfer of WS points if a pair or ice dance couple split up and form a new partnership.

These standings do not necessarily reflect the capabilities of the skater(s). Due to limits on entries to events (no more than three from each country), and varying numbers of high-level skaters in each country, skaters from some countries may find it more difficult to qualify to compete at major events. Thus, a skater with a lower SB but from a country with few high-level skaters may qualify to a major event while a skater with a much higher SB but from a country with more than three high-level skaters may not be sent. As a result, it is possible for a skater who regularly scores higher to end up with a much lower world standing.

The *season's world ranking* of a skater/couple is calculated similarly to the overall world standing but is based on the results of the ongoing season only.[[78]](#cite_note-isuws-79)

### Season's bests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Season's bests")]

The **season's best (SB)** of a skater/couple is the highest score achieved within a particular season. There is an SB for the combined total score and the individual segment scores (short program/rhythm dance, free skating/free dance). Only scores achieved at selected international competitions are considered; scores from national competitions and some international events are disregarded. The best combined total for each skater or couple appears on a list of season's bests,[[79]](#cite_note-isustats-80) and the list may be used to help determine participants in the following season's Grand Prix series.

Skaters and couples also have **personal best (PB)** scores, i.e. the highest scores achieved over their entire career, in terms of combined total and segment scores. However, PB scores are not completely comparable if achieved in different seasons because the ISU regulations and technical rules are modified before each new season.[[80]](#cite_note-81) There may be different requirements specified to achieve a certain level; the required elements may change and new elements may be allowed (for example, two quads in the short program were permitted starting in the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_figure_skating_season "2010–11 figure skating season")); and the point values may change (for example, the values of quads were increased after the [2010 Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics"), and a second step sequence is no longer assigned a level in the men's competition). As a result of these variations in the technical requirements, the ISU places more weight on the season's bests, which are fully comparable within any one season.

## Music and clothing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Music and clothing")]

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Music")]

For competitive programs, figure skaters were once restricted to instrumental music; vocals were allowed only if they contained no lyrics or words.[[81]](#cite_note-vocalsnote-82) Beginning in the 1997–98 season, the [ISU](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") decided to allow lyrics or words in [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance") music. Although the rules were not relaxed for singles and pairs, judges did not always penalize violations. At the [2011 World Championships](/wiki/2011_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2011 World Figure Skating Championships"), [Florent Amodio](/wiki/Florent_Amodio "Florent Amodio")'s long program music included words but an insufficient number of judges voted for a deduction.[[82]](#cite_note-w11mlp-83) In June 2012, the ISU voted to allow skaters from all disciplines to choose music with words in their competitive programs beginning in the [2014–15 season](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_figure_skating_season "2014–15 figure skating season").[[83]](#cite_note-NYT140218-84)[[84]](#cite_note-IN140716-85)

Skaters may use professional music editors so that their music meets requirements.[[85]](#cite_note-NDN140217-86) Ice dancers are required to skate to music that has a definite beat or rhythm. Singles and pair skaters more often skate to the melody and phrasing of their music. For [long programs](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating"), figure skaters generally search for music with different moods and tempos.[[86]](#cite_note-IN120423-87) Music selections for exhibitions are less constrained than for competitive programs.

### Clothing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Clothing")]

Main article: [Figure skating costume](/wiki/Figure_skating_costume "Figure skating costume")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Mone_Chiba_of_Japan_at_2025_Skate_Canada_International._01_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Mone_Chiba_of_Japan_at_2025_Skate_Canada_International._01_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mone_Chiba_of_Japan_at_2025_Skate_Canada_International._01_(cropped).jpg)

An example of women's figure skating costumes ([Mone Chiba](/wiki/Mone_Chiba "Mone Chiba") at the [2025 Skate Canada International](/wiki/2025_Skate_Canada_International "2025 Skate Canada International"))

Skaters are generally free to select their own attire, with a few restrictions. In competition, women may wear a dress, typically with matching attached briefs. This rule of costuming was created in response to [Katarina Witt's](/wiki/Katarina_Witt "Katarina Witt") costume and performance at the 1988 Winter Olympics.[[87]](#cite_note-88) In 2004, the rule was extended to allow women to wear trousers.[[88]](#cite_note-ST041205-89) They may wear opaque flesh-colored leggings or tights under dresses and skirts, which may extend to cover their skates. Men must wear trousers – they are not allowed to wear tights, although officials do not always impose a deduction for violations.[[89]](#cite_note-GS121031-90) Matching costumes are not required in pair skating and ice dance.[[90]](#cite_note-AS120818-91)

Competition costumes vary widely, from simple designs to heavily beaded or trimmed costumes. Skaters risk a deduction if a piece of their costume falls onto the ice surface. An official may stop a program if they deem there to be a hazard. Skaters and family members may design their own costumes, sometimes with assistance from their coach or choreographer, or turn to professional designers.[[90]](#cite_note-AS120818-91)[[91]](#cite_note-IN120820cos-92)[[92]](#cite_note-AS120914-93)[[93]](#cite_note-AS121004-94) Costumes may cost thousands of dollars if designed by a top-level costume maker.

According to current ISU regulations, costumes in competition must be fair, non-revealing, and appropriate for both short and long programs. Costumes should not be showy or exotic in nature. Clothing, however, can reflect the genre of music chosen.[[13]](#cite_note-isuSFTR-14) Although the use of flesh-colored fabric means the costumes are often less revealing than they may appear, there have been repeated attempts to ban clothing that gives the impression of "excessive nudity" or that is otherwise inappropriate for athletic competition.[[94]](#cite_note-usfsa99rb-95) In general, accessories or props are not permitted in competition.[[13]](#cite_note-isuSFTR-14) The ISU allowed an exception for the [original dance](/wiki/Original_dance "Original dance") in the 2007–08 season but not since.

## Eligibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Eligibility")]

### Age eligibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Age eligibility")]

To compete internationally on the senior level, skaters must be at least 17 before July 1 of the preceding year. To be eligible for junior-level events, a skater must be at least 13 but under 19 before that date (or 21 for male pair skaters and ice dancers of both genders).[[95]](#cite_note-espn-96)[[96]](#cite_note-isuage-97) A skater must meet the age requirement *before* it becomes July 1 in their place of birth. For example, [Adelina Sotnikova](/wiki/Adelina_Sotnikova "Adelina Sotnikova") was born a few hours into July 1, 1996, in Moscow and consequently, was not eligible to compete at Junior Worlds until 2011 and senior Worlds until 2013.[[97]](#cite_note-se1210-98) The ISU's rules apply to international events. Many countries have no age requirements for domestic non-ISU competitions, thus, some skaters compete at the senior level nationally while not eligible for international competition.

The [ISU](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") has modified its age rules several times. Before the 1990s, 12 was the minimum age for senior international competitions.[[98]](#cite_note-cbs980615-99) New rules were introduced in 1996, requiring skaters to be at least 15 before July 1 of the preceding year to compete at the Olympics, Worlds, Europeans, or Four Continents.[[95]](#cite_note-espn-96) The minimum age for all other senior internationals was 14 until July 2014, when it was raised to 15. The age limit was then raised to 16 years old for the 2023–24 season before increasing to 17 for the 2024–25 season, in a move to raise the age limit gradually.[[99]](#cite_note-100) The move came after the [2022 Winter Olympics scandal](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_singles "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Women's singles") over [Kamila Valieva's](/wiki/Kamila_Valieva "Kamila Valieva") doping allegations and the controversy over her responsibility as a minor.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

During the 2005–06 season, [Mao Asada](/wiki/Mao_Asada "Mao Asada") of Japan was age-eligible to compete at the Grand Prix Final, where she claimed the title, but she was not permitted to compete at the Olympics. For the [2008 World Championships](/wiki/2008_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2008 World Figure Skating Championships"), the United States was obliged to send skaters who had placed 5th and 7th at nationals because higher-placed skaters were too young, including a skater who missed the cutoff by 20 days.[[95]](#cite_note-espn-96)[[101]](#cite_note-agereq-102) The ISU has strictly enforced the rules in recent years.[[95]](#cite_note-espn-96) However, American pair skater [Natasha Kuchiki](/wiki/Natasha_Kuchiki "Natasha Kuchiki") was allowed to compete at the [1990 World Championships](/wiki/1990_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1990 World Figure Skating Championships") when she was two years too young and American single skater [Tara Lipinski](/wiki/Tara_Lipinski "Tara Lipinski"), who was 13 at the time the 1996 rules were introduced, was [grandfathered](/wiki/Grandfather_clause "Grandfather clause") into remaining eligible for future events, along with other skaters who had already competed at the World Championships. A loophole also existed for a few years for underage skaters who had medaled at Junior Worlds.[[102]](#cite_note-cbs000316-103)

As in [gymnastics](/wiki/Age_requirements_in_gymnastics#Age_falsification "Age requirements in gymnastics"), skating has experienced controversy surrounding possible [age falsification](/wiki/Age_fabrication "Age fabrication"). On February 14, 2011, questions emerged surrounding nine Chinese skaters. The [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") found that birthdates listed on the [Chinese Skating Association](/wiki/Chinese_Skating_Association "Chinese Skating Association")'s website suggested five female skaters, [Sui Wenjing](/wiki/Sui_Wenjing "Sui Wenjing"), [Zhang Dan](/wiki/Zhang_Dan "Zhang Dan"), [Yu Xiaoyu](/wiki/Yu_Xiaoyu "Yu Xiaoyu"), [Geng Bingwa](/wiki/Geng_Bingwa "Geng Bingwa"), and [Xu Binshu](/wiki/Xu_Binshu "Xu Binshu"), were younger than their ISU ages, and six male skaters, [Han Cong](/wiki/Han_Cong "Han Cong"), [Zhang Hao](/wiki/Zhang_Hao_(figure_skater) "Zhang Hao (figure skater)"), [Yan Han](/wiki/Yan_Han_(figure_skater) "Yan Han (figure skater)") and [Gao Yu](/w/index.php?title=Gao_Yu_(figure_skater)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gao Yu (figure skater) (page does not exist)"), Zang Wenbo, and Xu Zuoren were older.[[95]](#cite_note-espn-96)[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105) The dates disappeared from the website by February 15.[[105]](#cite_note-feb1711-106) On February 17, the ISU said there were no discrepancies for [Zhang Dan](/wiki/Zhang_Dan "Zhang Dan"), [Zhang Hao](/wiki/Zhang_Hao_(figure_skater) "Zhang Hao (figure skater)"), and [Xu Binshu](/wiki/Xu_Binshu "Xu Binshu") between the birthdates listed on their passports, ISU registration forms and the [Chinese Olympic Committee](/wiki/Chinese_Olympic_Committee "Chinese Olympic Committee")'s website.[[105]](#cite_note-feb1711-106) Athletes in China sometimes face pressure to falsify their age.[[106]](#cite_note-survive-107)

### Other eligibility rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Other eligibility rules")]

Skaters may represent a country of which they are not yet a citizen in most competitions, except the Olympics which require citizenship.

At most international events, each country may send one to a maximum of three entries per discipline. Consequently, even if a skater has a high season's best, he or she may not be sent to major events if their country has many good skaters in their discipline. Some skaters have tried to circumvent this by representing another country. In response, the ISU introduced rules barring skaters from international events for a certain period of time. In the 2010 regulations, it was 24 months or more from the date of the last ISU Championship.[[107]](#cite_note-genreg2010-108) In the 2012 regulations, the minimum was 18 months for singles and 12 months for pairs/ice dancers from the date of their last ISU Championships (Worlds, Europeans, Four Continents, Junior Worlds) and 12 months if they competed in some other international competition.[[108]](#cite_note-genreg2012-109) Competitors may sit out for much longer because they also have to obtain a release from their previous federation. The ISU has set no limit to how long a country may hold skaters.[[108]](#cite_note-genreg2012-109)

Skaters may lose their ISU eligibility if they perform in an unsanctioned show or competition.

Beginning in the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_figure_skating_season "2010–11 figure skating season"), minimum scores were introduced for the World, European, or Four Continents Championships. In the [2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_figure_skating_season "2011–12 figure skating season") season, different minimum scores were introduced for the Grand Prix series.

## Competitors' expenses, income, and funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Competitors' expenses, income, and funding")]

Figure skating is an expensive sport.[[109]](#cite_note-usfscost-110)[[110]](#cite_note-nyt32611-111)[[111]](#cite_note-EJ130311-112)[[112]](#cite_note-tie110920-113) This is particularly due to the costs of ice time and coaching.[[113]](#cite_note-nyt991219-114) In the late 1980s, the expenses of a top-ten women's competitor at the U.S. Championships reached nearly [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")50,000 a year.[[114]](#cite_note-SS880228-115) In October 2004, a [U.S. Figure Skating](/wiki/U.S._Figure_Skating "U.S. Figure Skating") article estimated the annual expense at US$9,000–$10,000 for pre-juvenile, US$18,000 for juvenile, US$35,000–$40,000 for novice, and said junior and senior levels were somewhat more expensive.[[109]](#cite_note-usfscost-110) In the 2010s, American senior national medalists had expenses in the mid-five-figure range.[[110]](#cite_note-nyt32611-111)[[115]](#cite_note-SI130410-116) Swiss skater [Stéphane Lambiel](/wiki/St%C3%A9phane_Lambiel "Stéphane Lambiel") said his costs were around [CHF](/wiki/Swiss_franc "Swiss franc") 100,000 per season.[[116]](#cite_note-AS120402-117) World champion [Patrick Chan](/wiki/Patrick_Chan "Patrick Chan")'s expenses were [Can$](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar")150,000.[[117]](#cite_note-embracing-118) In 2015, [CBC Sports](/wiki/CBC_Sports "CBC Sports") estimated that a Canadian pair team had expenses of about Can$100,000 per year.[[118]](#cite_note-CBC151118b-119)

Prize money is relatively low compared to other sports.[[119]](#cite_note-ct111028-120) A men's or women's singles skater who won the [2011 World Championships](/wiki/2011_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2011 World Figure Skating Championships") earned US$45,000,[[120]](#cite_note-isuprev110423-121) about 1.8% to 2.5% of the US$1,800,000–$2,400,000 for winners of the tennis [US Open](/wiki/2011_US_Open_(tennis) "2011 US Open (tennis)") and [Australian Open](/wiki/2012_Australian_Open "2012 Australian Open").[[121]](#cite_note-usopen-122)[[122]](#cite_note-ap120123-123) A couple who won the pairs or ice dance title split US$67,500.[[120]](#cite_note-isuprev110423-121) A winner of the senior [Grand Prix Final](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "2011–12 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") in December 2011 earned US$25,000.[[123]](#cite_note-isu111207-124)

Some national associations provide funding to some skaters if they meet certain criteria.[[124]](#cite_note-usfsfunding-125) Many skaters take part-time jobs and some have tried [crowdfunding](/wiki/Crowdfunding "Crowdfunding").[[125]](#cite_note-IN120531-126)[[126]](#cite_note-CBC151118-127) In Germany, many elite skaters join the army to fund their skating.[[127]](#cite_note-ifs110713-128) In Italy, some skaters join police agencies' sport groups, such as the [Polizia Penitenziaria](/wiki/Polizia_Penitenziaria "Polizia Penitenziaria")'s *[Fiamme Azzurre](/wiki/Gruppo_Sportivo_Fiamme_Azzurre "Gruppo Sportivo Fiamme Azzurre")* ([Carolina Kostner](/wiki/Carolina_Kostner "Carolina Kostner"), [Anna Cappellini](/wiki/Anna_Cappellini "Anna Cappellini"), [Luca Lanotte](/wiki/Luca_Lanotte "Luca Lanotte"))[[128]](#cite_note-peniten-129)[[129]](#cite_note-peniten2-130) or [Polizia di Stato](/wiki/Polizia_di_Stato "Polizia di Stato")'s *Fiamme Oro* ([Federica Faiella](/wiki/Federica_Faiella "Federica Faiella"), [Paolo Bacchini](/wiki/Paolo_Bacchini "Paolo Bacchini")).[[130]](#cite_note-polstato-131) Some competitive skaters depend on income from shows.[[131]](#cite_note-dilemma-132)[[132]](#cite_note-promin-133) Shows must be sanctioned by their association, i.e. skaters may lose their competitive eligibility if they take part without permission. In some cases, skaters may feel pressure to compete through injury to be allowed to perform in a show.[[131]](#cite_note-dilemma-132)
Others may become involved with coaching younger athletes in order to fund their own training costs.

## Injuries and health issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Injuries and health issues")]

In some countries, medical personnel may be slow to respond to accidents. At the [2000 World Championships](/wiki/2000_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2000 World Figure Skating Championships") in Nice, France, a pair skater who had been injured in a lift accident lay on the ice for several minutes and had to get up and leave the ice on his own before being offered medical attention.[[133]](#cite_note-AP000329op-134)

### Head injuries, falls and collisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Head injuries, falls and collisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Yuzuru_Hanyu_at_2014_Cup_of_China.jpg/250px-Yuzuru_Hanyu_at_2014_Cup_of_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yuzuru_Hanyu_at_2014_Cup_of_China.jpg)

[Yuzuru Hanyu](/wiki/Yuzuru_Hanyu "Yuzuru Hanyu") competes with his head bandaged after his accident at the [2014 Cup of China](/wiki/2014_Cup_of_China "2014 Cup of China")

Competitive skaters generally do not wear helmets or other protective gear. There is a risk of head injuries, particularly in [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating") as a result of falls from lifts.[[134]](#cite_note-cbs991017-135)[[135]](#cite_note-binnebose-136) Although pair skaters are most susceptible, serious head injuries can occur in all disciplines, including ice dance.[[136]](#cite_note-IN160202-137)[[137]](#cite_note-IN120713dl-138)[[138]](#cite_note-abssk2010-139) Partners have accidentally slashed each other with their skate blades.[[139]](#cite_note-batessamuelson-140)[[140]](#cite_note-icenetfeb12-141) This may occur when partners drift too close during side-by-side [camel spins](/wiki/Camel_spins "Camel spins"). Several female pair skaters have suffered head/face injuries during this element, including [Elena Berezhnaya](/wiki/Elena_Berezhnaya "Elena Berezhnaya"),[[141]](#cite_note-comeback-142) [Tatiana Totmianina](/wiki/Tatiana_Totmianina "Tatiana Totmianina"),[[142]](#cite_note-AP10232004-143) [Jessica Dubé](/wiki/Jessica_Dub%C3%A9 "Jessica Dubé"),[[143]](#cite_note-cnn070423-144) [Mandy Wötzel](/wiki/Mandy_W%C3%B6tzel "Mandy Wötzel"),[[144]](#cite_note-nytjl980204-145) [Galina Maniachenko (Efremenko)](/wiki/Galina_Efremenko "Galina Efremenko"),[[145]](#cite_note-maniachenko-146) and [Elena Riabchuk](/wiki/Elena_Riabchuk "Elena Riabchuk").[[146]](#cite_note-rzinj-147)

Commenting on falls and concussions, [Madison Hubbell](/wiki/Madison_Hubbell "Madison Hubbell") said that "Most of the time, the worst falls are on things we kind of take for granted."[[136]](#cite_note-IN160202-137) [Shin splints](/wiki/Shin_splints "Shin splints"),[[147]](#cite_note-SM1404-148) knee injuries, and back problems are not uncommon.[[6]](#cite_note-NYT090622-7)[[148]](#cite_note-gs4202-149)[[149]](#cite_note-podtod-150) Hip damage may occur as a result of practising jumps and throws.[[6]](#cite_note-NYT090622-7)[[150]](#cite_note-taralip-151) In rare cases, intensive training of spins may result in subtle concussions ([Lucinda Ruh](/wiki/Lucinda_Ruh "Lucinda Ruh")).[[151]](#cite_note-spincon-152)[[152]](#cite_note-NBCC140212-153)

Injuries have also been sustained by skaters from different teams when many skaters are practising on the ice.[[153]](#cite_note-densta-154) [Midori Ito](/wiki/Midori_Ito "Midori Ito") collided with [Laetitia Hubert](/wiki/Laetitia_Hubert "Laetitia Hubert") at the 1991 World Championships, while [Oksana Baiul](/wiki/Oksana_Baiul "Oksana Baiul") and [Tanja Szewczenko](/wiki/Tanja_Szewczenko "Tanja Szewczenko") collided at the 1994 Olympics, but all went on to compete. At the 2014 [Cup of China](/wiki/Cup_of_China "Cup of China") the Japanese [Yuzuru Hanyu](/wiki/Yuzuru_Hanyu "Yuzuru Hanyu"), winner of the [Olympic title](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_singles "Figure skating at the 2014 Winter Olympics – Men's singles") a few months before, and the Chinese [Han Yan](/wiki/Yan_Han_(figure_skater) "Yan Han (figure skater)"), collided during the warm up of the free program. Despite being visibly injured, both skaters finished the competition.[[154]](#cite_note-CBCCA11082014-155) On practice sessions with multiple skaters on the ice, the skater whose music is playing conventionally has right of way. Also, pairs and ice dancers skating as a unit have right of way over those skating separately as changing course is more difficult for a couple.

### Eating disorders and RED–S

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Eating disorders and RED–S")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Gold_-_2016_Four_Continents_-_2.jpg/250px-Gold_-_2016_Four_Continents_-_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gold_-_2016_Four_Continents_-_2.jpg)

[Gracie Gold](/wiki/Gracie_Gold "Gracie Gold") at the [2016 Four Continents Championship](/wiki/2016_Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "2016 Four Continents Figure Skating Championships").

[Eating disorders](/wiki/Eating_disorders "Eating disorders") are reportedly common in figure skating[[155]](#cite_note-HP100120-156)[[156]](#cite_note-HP100121-157)[[157]](#cite_note-muscles-158) and can result in the development of [relative energy deficiency in sport](/wiki/Relative_energy_deficiency_in_sport "Relative energy deficiency in sport") (RED-S), formerly known as the "female athlete triad". RED-S is a syndrome of three interrelated conditions which can cause long-standing illness in girls and women and even death. Body image and the need to maintain a fit body is a very common issue in figure skating, as skaters age, their bodies change and change the way they must approach the sport.[[158]](#cite_note-159) Skaters such as [Gracie Gold](/wiki/Gracie_Gold "Gracie Gold")[[159]](#cite_note-160) and [Ashley Wagner](/wiki/Ashley_Wagner "Ashley Wagner")[[160]](#cite_note-161) have faced issues such as eating disorders and depression.

### Doping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Doping")]

Figure skaters occasionally have positive [doping](/wiki/Doping_in_figure_skating "Doping in figure skating") results but it is not common.[[161]](#cite_note-WADA-162) In a 1991 interview, three-time Olympic champion [Irina Rodnina](/wiki/Irina_Rodnina "Irina Rodnina") admitted that Soviet skaters used doping substances in preparation for the competitive season, stating: "Boys in pairs and singles used drugs, but this was only in August or September. This was done just in training, and everyone was tested (in the Soviet Union) before competitions."[[162]](#cite_note-CT910214-163)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of figure skating](/wiki/History_of_figure_skating "History of figure skating")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Jackson_Haines2.jpg/250px-Jackson_Haines2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jackson_Haines2.jpg)

[Jackson Haines](/wiki/Jackson_Haines "Jackson Haines") is considered the father of modern figure skating.

Although people have been [ice skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating") for centuries, figure skating in its current form originated in the mid-19th century. *A Treatise on Skating* (1772) by the accomplished skater, Welshman Lt. 'Captain' [Robert Jones](/wiki/Robert_Jones_(artilleryman) "Robert Jones (artilleryman)") (c. 1740 – c. 1788), is the first-known book on figure skating. He designed skates that could be attached to shoes by screws through the heels (rather than using straps), and these were soon available from Riccard's Manufactory in London.[[163]](#cite_note-164)

Competitions were held in the "English style" of skating, which was stiff and formal and bore very little resemblance to modern figure skating. Without changing the basic techniques used by skaters, only a limited number of figure skating moves could be performed. This was still true in the mid-1800s before improvements were brought about by American skater [Jackson Haines](/wiki/Jackson_Haines "Jackson Haines"), who was considered to be the "father of modern figure skating". In the mid-1860s, Haines introduced a new style of skating, incorporating free and expressive techniques, which became known as the "international style". Although popular in Europe, the international style of skating was not widely adopted in the United States until long after Haines's death.[[164]](#cite_note-Haines-165)

### Early 1900s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Early 1900s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Special_figures_by_Panin.svg/250px-Special_figures_by_Panin.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Special_figures_by_Panin.svg)

[Special figures](/wiki/Special_figures "Special figures") by [Nikolai Panin](/wiki/Nikolai_Panin "Nikolai Panin") at the [1908 Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1908 Summer Olympics")

The [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") was founded in 1892. The first [European Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/European_Figure_Skating_Championships "European Figure Skating Championships") were held in 1891 in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), Germany (won by [Oskar Uhlig](/wiki/Oskar_Uhlig "Oskar Uhlig")), and the first [World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") were held in 1896 in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), Russia (won by [Gilbert Fuchs](/wiki/Gilbert_Fuchs "Gilbert Fuchs")). Only men competed in the early events but in 1902 a woman entered the World Championships for the first time: British female skater [Madge Syers](/wiki/Madge_Syers "Madge Syers") competed in the men's competition, finishing in second place behind Sweden's [Ulrich Salchow](/wiki/Ulrich_Salchow "Ulrich Salchow"). The ISU quickly banned women from competing against men, and established a separate "ladies" competition in [1906](/wiki/1906_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1906 World Figure Skating Championships"). [Pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating") was introduced at the [1908 World Championships](/wiki/1908_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1908 World Figure Skating Championships"), where the title was won by [Anna Hübler](/wiki/Anna_H%C3%BCbler "Anna Hübler") and [Heinrich Burger](/wiki/Heinrich_Burger "Heinrich Burger") of Germany.

Figure skating was the first winter sport contested at the Olympics; it made its Olympic debut at the [1908 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1908 Summer Olympics") in London.[[1]](#cite_note-AFS2014-1)[[165]](#cite_note-ISUHistory-166) On March 20, 1914, an international figure skating championship was held in [New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut"). This event was the forerunner of both the [United States](/wiki/U.S._Figure_Skating_Championships "U.S. Figure Skating Championships") and [Canadian National Championships](/wiki/Canadian_Figure_Skating_Championships "Canadian Figure Skating Championships"). However, international competitions in figure skating were interrupted by [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").

In the 1920s and 1930s, figure skating was dominated by [Sonja Henie](/wiki/Sonja_Henie "Sonja Henie") of Norway. Henie turned competitive success into a lucrative professional career as a movie star and touring skater, also setting the fashion for female skaters to wear short skirts and white boots.[[166]](#cite_note-spillhenie-167) The top male figure skaters of this period included Sweden's [Gillis Grafström](/wiki/Gillis_Grafstr%C3%B6m "Gillis Grafström") and Austria's [Karl Schäfer](/wiki/Karl_Sch%C3%A4fer_(figure_skater) "Karl Schäfer (figure skater)").

### After World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: After World War II")]

Skating competitions were again interrupted for several years by [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). After the war, with many European rinks in ruins, skaters from the United States and Canada began to dominate international competitions and to introduce technical innovations to the sport. [Dick Button](/wiki/Dick_Button "Dick Button"), 1948 and 1952 Olympic Champion, was the first skater to perform the double Axel and triple loop jumps, as well as the flying camel spin.

The World Figure Skating Championships did not include [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance") until [1952](/wiki/1952_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1952 World Figure Skating Championships").[[165]](#cite_note-ISUHistory-166) In its early years, ice dance was dominated by British skaters, and until 1960 the world title was won every year by a British couple, beginning with [Jean Westwood](/wiki/Jean_Westwood_(figure_skater) "Jean Westwood (figure skater)") and [Lawrence Demmy](/wiki/Lawrence_Demmy "Lawrence Demmy").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011102-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-G0313-0017-001%2C_Ludmilla_Beloussowa%2C_Oleg_Protopopow.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-G0313-0017-001%2C_Ludmilla_Beloussowa%2C_Oleg_Protopopow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-G0313-0017-001,_Ludmilla_Beloussowa,_Oleg_Protopopow.jpg)

Russian pair skaters [Ludmila Belousova](/wiki/Ludmila_Belousova "Ludmila Belousova") and [Oleg Protopopov](/wiki/Oleg_Protopopov "Oleg Protopopov") in 1968

On February 15, 1961, the entire U.S. figure skating team and their coaches were killed in the crash of [Sabena Flight 548](/wiki/Sabena_Flight_548 "Sabena Flight 548") in [Brussels, Belgium](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") en route to the World Championships in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). This tragedy sent the U.S. skating program into a period of rebuilding.

Meanwhile, the Soviet Union rose to become a dominant force in the sport, especially in the disciplines of pair skating and ice dance. Soviet and Russian domination in pair skating began in the 1950s and continued throughout the rest of the 1900s. Only five non-Soviet or Russian teams won the Olympics and World Championships from 1965 to 2010.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011191-168) When [Shen Xue](/wiki/Shen_Xue "Shen Xue") and [Zhao Hongbo](/wiki/Zhao_Hongbo "Zhao Hongbo") of China won the gold medal at the 2010 games, this was the first time since 1960 that a Russian, Soviet, or Unified Team (CIS) flagged team did not win the gold medal.[[168]](#cite_note-169)[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171)[[171]](#cite_note-172) The [1967 World Championships](/wiki/1967_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1967 World Figure Skating Championships") was the last event held on an outdoor rink.[[172]](#cite_note-173)

### Effect of television and the present day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Effect of television and the present day")]

[Compulsory figures](/wiki/Compulsory_figures "Compulsory figures") formerly accounted for up to 60% of the score in singles figure skating,[[173]](#cite_note-CBS981216-174) meaning that skaters who could build up a significant lead in figures could win competitions even if they were mediocre free skaters. As television coverage of skating events became more important, the popularity of free skating increased because this part of the competition was televised and shown to the general public, whereas the compulsory figures competition was not. The television audience would complain when superior free programs sometimes failed to equate to gold medal victories.[[174]](#cite_note-:0-175) Beginning in 1968, the ISU progressively reduced the [weighting](/wiki/Weighting "Weighting") of compulsory figures and introduced the short program in 1973.[[173]](#cite_note-CBS981216-174) A critical issue was said to have been the continued failure of [Janet Lynn](/wiki/Janet_Lynn "Janet Lynn") to achieve on the world stage despite her outstanding free skate programs. For example, she missed out on a podium place at the [1971 World Championships](/wiki/1971_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1971 World Figure Skating Championships") after winning the free skate competition decisively, which produced an uproar and loud booing from the audience during the medal ceremony.[[174]](#cite_note-:0-175)

With these changes, the emphasis in competitive figure skating shifted to increased athleticism. Landing triple jumps during the short program and the free skate became more important. By the 1980s, some skaters began practising [quadruple jumps](/wiki/Quad_(figure_skating) "Quad (figure skating)"). [Jozef Sabovčík](/wiki/Jozef_Sabov%C4%8D%C3%ADk "Jozef Sabovčík") of Czechoslovakia landed a quad [toe loop](/wiki/Toe_loop_jump "Toe loop jump") at the [1986 European Championships](/wiki/1986_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "1986 European Figure Skating Championships") which was recognized at the event but then ruled invalid three weeks later due to a touchdown with his free foot.[[175]](#cite_note-quadevo991202-176) At the [1988 World Championships](/wiki/1988_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1988 World Figure Skating Championships"), [Kurt Browning](/wiki/Kurt_Browning "Kurt Browning") of Canada landed the first quad toe loop which has remained ratified.[[176]](#cite_note-nyt880326-177) Despite expectations, it was several years before quads became an important part of men's skating.[[175]](#cite_note-quadevo991202-176) In 1988, Japan's [Midori Ito](/wiki/Midori_Ito "Midori Ito") became the first woman to land a triple Axel, pushing the athletic and technical level for women's programs. Worth only 20% by 1989, compulsory figures were eliminated from international competition in 1990.[[173]](#cite_note-CBS981216-174)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Takahiko_Kozuka_with_Nobuo_Sato_kiss_%26_cry_2008-2009_GPF.jpg/250px-Takahiko_Kozuka_with_Nobuo_Sato_kiss_%26_cry_2008-2009_GPF.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takahiko_Kozuka_with_Nobuo_Sato_kiss_%26_cry_2008-2009_GPF.jpg)

[Takahiko Kozuka](/wiki/Takahiko_Kozuka "Takahiko Kozuka") waits for his marks with coach [Nobuo Sato](/wiki/Nobuo_Sato "Nobuo Sato") in the "[Kiss and cry](/wiki/Kiss_and_cry "Kiss and cry")" area.

Television contributed to the sport's popularity by showing skaters in the [kiss and cry](/wiki/Kiss_and_cry "Kiss and cry") area after competing.[[177]](#cite_note-macur20100222-178) Television also played a role in removing the restrictive [amateur](/wiki/Amateurism "Amateurism") status rules that once governed the sport. In May 1990, the ISU voted to allow skaters intending to skate professionally to return to ISU competition, provided that they obtained their national association's permission.[[178]](#cite_note-nyt900502-179) In 1995, in an effort to retain skaters who might otherwise have given up their eligibility to participate in lucrative professional events, the ISU introduced prize money at its major competitions, funded by revenues from selling the TV rights to those events.[[179]](#cite_note-180)

In 1984, more than 24 million people in Great Britain watched ice dance pair [Jayne Torvill](/wiki/Jayne_Torvill "Jayne Torvill") and [Christopher Dean](/wiki/Christopher_Dean "Christopher Dean") earn unanimous 6.0s for presentation, the only perfect score in Olympic skating history, which was ranked the 8th greatest sporting moment in a UK poll.[[180]](#cite_note-BBC840214-181)[[181]](#cite_note-CH4GSM-182) In 1988, East German [Katarina Witt](/wiki/Katarina_Witt "Katarina Witt") became only the second woman in figure skating history, after Norwegian [Sonja Henie](/wiki/Sonja_Henie "Sonja Henie"), to defend her Olympic title successfully. [*Time*](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") magazine called her "the most beautiful face of socialism."[[182]](#cite_note-183) In the 1993 National Sports Study II, considered by the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") as the largest study of spectator sport popularity in America, women's figure skating was the second most popular spectator sport in America, just behind [NFL football](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") out of over 100 sports surveyed.[[183]](#cite_note-1993nssII-184) The 1993 study found that three figure skaters – [Dorothy Hamill](/wiki/Dorothy_Hamill "Dorothy Hamill"), [Peggy Fleming](/wiki/Peggy_Fleming "Peggy Fleming"), and [Scott Hamilton](/wiki/Scott_Hamilton_(figure_skater) "Scott Hamilton (figure skater)")[[184]](#cite_note-lvsun-185) – were among the eight most popular athletes in the United States, of more than 800 athletes surveyed.[[185]](#cite_note-iebrennan-186) The [Tonya Harding](/wiki/Tonya_Harding "Tonya Harding") scandal in 1994 increased interest in figure skating.[[186]](#cite_note-nyt940206-187) The first night of the women's figure skating competition in the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics") achieved higher [Nielsen TV ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings") than the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXVIII "Super Bowl XXVIII") three weeks earlier and, to that date, was the most watched sports television program in the US.[[187]](#cite_note-usatslip-188)

To show support, spectators sometimes throw a variety of items onto the ice after the end of a figure skating program, most commonly stuffed toys and flowers. Officials discourage people from throwing flowers that are not fully wrapped because of the possibility of debris disrupting or endangering the following skaters.[[188]](#cite_note-TGAM130119-189)[[189]](#cite_note-LFP130308-190)

Countries that have produced a great many successful skaters include Russia and the former Soviet Union, the United States, Canada, Japan, China, France, Germany, and Italy. While the sport has grown in East Asia, training opportunities in South Asia are limited due to a scarcity of ice rinks. India had only four major indoor ice rinks as of 2011, but there were plans for ten more to be built, mostly in malls, over the following five years.[[190]](#cite_note-India-191) As of 2016, three of these intended ten indoor rinks were built at Neptune Magnet Mall,[[191]](#cite_note-192) Atria Millennium Mall,[[192]](#cite_note-193) and Lulu Mall[[193]](#cite_note-194) respectively.

[Four skating](/wiki/Four_skating "Four skating") has mostly disappeared, while [synchronized skating](/wiki/Synchronized_skating "Synchronized skating"), singles/pair skating and ice dance have grown. On April 6, 2011, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") officially confirmed the approval of a figure skating team event, which was introduced at the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Team_event "Figure skating at the 2014 Winter Olympics – Team event").[[194]](#cite_note-reu110406-195) The elimination of the [compulsory dance](/wiki/Compulsory_dance_(ice_dance) "Compulsory dance (ice dance)") segment provided space for the team event.[[195]](#cite_note-reu120329-196) Each team is composed of a men's and women's singles skater, a pair, and an ice dance duo. A maximum of ten teams can compete, with five eliminated after the short program.[[196]](#cite_note-ctteam-197) In December 2011, the ISU released details of the qualifying system and the competition.[[197]](#cite_note-isu9861-198)

According to writer Ellyn Kestnbaum, television helped figure skating become more celebrity oriented, with high-profile skaters being treated like entertainers and famous athletes in other sports.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKestnbaum2003vii-199) She states that television has encouraged "casual fans and other members of the general public to understand skating in terms of media-shaped parables about broader cultural issues,"[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKestnbaum2003vii-199) including anxieties about femininity or masculinity, individuality versus conformity, and nationalistic sporting contests. Kestnbaum also states that even though most skating broadcasts are produced by networks' sports divisions, competitions, even the more serious ones, they are packaged with "more emphasis on the aesthetic qualities of the skating—or of the female skaters—and on the pleasures of rooting for a home-country hero than on the technical details that determine the winners".[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKestnbaum2003vii-199) Viewers who depend upon the televised coverage of figure skating are limited in their access to information about it as a sport because broadcasters present a week's worth of competition in a few hours and they are compelled to avoid overloading viewers with too much information about the more technical aspects of the sport.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKestnbaum2003vii-199) Journalists and scholars who write about figure skating also tend to focus on the same broader cultural issues.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKestnbaum2003viii-200)

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Figure_skating&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Literature")]

* The *[Prose Edda](/wiki/Prose_Edda "Prose Edda")* (c. 1220), an [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") textbook and compilation of [Norse mythology](/wiki/Norse_mythology "Norse mythology") that mentions ice skating, written in [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") during the early 13th century.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xix-201)
* *The Art of Skating*, [Robert Jones](/wiki/Robert_Jones_(artilleryman) "Robert Jones (artilleryman)") (1772), the earliest book about figure skating.[[201]](#cite_note-whoswho-202)[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xx-203)
* *Le vrai pattineur* (*The True Skater*), Jean Garcin (1813), the first book about ice skating published in France.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xx-203)
* *The Art of Skating*, George Anderson (1852), about skating in England and Scotland.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines201123-204)
* *The Skater's Manual*, Edward F. Gill (1863), first book about skating written in North America.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xx-203)
* *Figure Skating*, H.E. Vandervell and T. Maxwell Witham (1869), the first book to refer to the sport of "figure skating".[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xx-203)
* *Spuren auf dem Eise* (*Tracings on the Ice*), 1881. Written by three members of the Vienna Skating Club, it described the Viennese style of skating and was the most extensive technical book about figure skating published up to that time.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xx-203) A second expanded edition, which included descriptions of ice dances popular in the 1880s, was published in 1892.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xx-203)[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines201161-205)
* *The Art of Skating*, [Irving Brokaw](/wiki/Irving_Brokaw "Irving Brokaw") (1910). The first of four books about figure skating, all with the same title. Contributors of the first book included leading skaters of the time, including [Georg Sanders](/wiki/Georg_Sanders "Georg Sanders") of Russia, who wrote about special figures, [Phyllis Johnson](/wiki/Phyllis_Johnson "Phyllis Johnson") and [James H. Johnson](/wiki/James_H._Johnson_(figure_skater) "James H. Johnson (figure skater)") from England, who wrote about pair skating, and [Gilbert Fuchs](/wiki/Gilbert_Fuchs "Gilbert Fuchs") from Germany, who wrote an essay entitled, "Theory of Skating".[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines201144-206)
* *Modern Figure Skating*, T.D. Richardson (1938). Includes list of jumps created up to that time.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xxiv-207)
* *Wings on My Feet*, Sonja Henie (1940).[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines20112-208)
* *Ice Skating: A History*, Nigel Brown (1959). First comprehensive history of figure skating.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xxv-209)
* *75 Years of European and World Championships* (1967). ISU publication to commemorate its 75th anniversary.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xxvi-210)
* *Our Skating Heritage*, Dennis Bird (1979). History of the National Skating Association in England, to commemorate its 100th anniversary.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xxvi-210)
* *Skating in America: The 75th Anniversary History of the United Figure Skating Association*, Benjamin T. Wright (1996).[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHines2011xxviii-211)
